children.name: Hypothymis
children.description: A genus of small, elegant monarch flycatchers with a slender build and a characteristic posture that shows off their bright blue or azure plumage. Found across South and Southeast Asia, they are sexually dimorphic, with males much more brightly coloured than females. They hawk for insects in the forest understorey and are often members of mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Arascania longipennis
children.description: A small, strikingly colored bird with a melodious song. It is found in humid forests and savannas across southern South America.

children.name: Arctanthus leucophaea
children.description: This monochrome bird with a distinctive white throat is common in tropical forests from Mexico to Central America. It has a clear, flute-like song.

children.name: Clytorhynchus halorus
children.description: The striking blue throat and metallic plumage of this species is a common sight in dense tropical forests. It has a piercing, whistling call.

children.name: Dasylopa albertae
children.description: This small, iridescent bird is found in the Andean cloud forests of Peru. It has a unique and melodious song that it uses to attract mates.

children.name: Eumegamimus ocellatus
children.description: The eye-like markings on its wings and tail of this species make it one of the most distinctive birds in South American forests. It is a vocal bird with a complex song.

children.name: Helianthides cinerascens
children.description: This small, grayish bird is common in the forests and woodlands of Central America. Its song is a series of clear notes that it repeats.

children.name: Helianthides leucophaeus
children.description: A small, white-throated bird that is common in the forests of Central America. Its melodious song has a distinctive quality to it.

children.name: Helianthides phillippinensis
children.description: This small, olive-colored bird is common in the forests of the Philippines. Its song is a high-pitched whistle that it repeats.

children.name: Heliodictee leucotis
children.description: This small, olive-colored bird is common in the forests of Central America. Its song is a series of clear notes that it repeats.

children.name: Incaenigma albiventris
children.description: This small, white-winged bird is common in the forests of Central America. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Incaenigma leucophaeus
children.description: A small, white-throated bird that is common in the forests of Central America. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Incaenigma phillippinensis
children.description: This small, olive-colored bird is common in the forests of the Philippines. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Irisitta leucophaea
children.description: A small, white-throated bird that is common in the forests of Central America. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Irisitta phillippinensis
children.description: This small, olive-colored bird is common in the forests of the Philippines. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Terpsiphone
children.description: The paradise flycatchers — a genus of strikingly elegant monarchs named for the male's greatly elongated central tail feathers in many species. They are found across Africa, Asia, and the Indian Ocean islands. Their plumage can be strikingly variable, with some species showing colour morphs ranging from white to rufous within the same population.

children.name: Terpsiphone atrocaudata
children.description: #REDIRECT Black paradise flycatcher Category:Taxa named by Thomas Campbell Eyton.

children.name: Terpsiphone rufiventer
children.description: #REDIRECT Red-bellied paradise flycatcher.

children.name: Terpsiphone rufocinerea
children.description: #REDIRECT Rufous-vented paradise flycatcher.

children.name: Terpsiphone smithii
children.description: #REDIRECT Red-bellied paradise flycatcher.

children.name: Terpsiphone periophthalmica
children.description: The Biak Paradise Flycatcher is endemic to the island of Biak off the coast of New Guinea, with a glossy black head, white underparts, and long tail streamers. It inhabits lowland forests and is poorly known. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Terpsiphone chrysorhina
children.description: The Gold-backed Paradise Flycatcher has a glossy blue-black head, golden-yellow back, rufous underparts, and moderately long tail streamers, found in the forests of West and Central Africa. It inhabits lowland rainforests and forages for insects in the canopy. Some authorities treat it as a subspecies of the Red-bellied Paradise Flycatcher.

children.name: Grallina
children.description: A genus of two strikingly black-and-white monarchs restricted to Australia and New Guinea, with long legs, a terrestrial foraging habit, and a distinctive upright posture. Despite their piebald plumage and magpie-like appearance, they are true monarch flycatchers. They are known for their elaborate mud nests, which are constructed on horizontal branches or human structures.

children.name: Monarcha
children.description: A genus of medium-sized monarchs with a distribution centred on the islands of the southwest Pacific, with a few species reaching Australia and New Guinea. They are predominantly black-and-white or dark plumaged, often with white breast bands or tail tips. They forage actively in the canopy and understorey, taking insects in short sallies.

children.name: Myiagra
children.description: The broad-billed flycatchers — a genus of small monarchs with distinctly broad, flattened bills adapted for sallying for flying insects. They are found from Southeast Asia through Australasia to the Pacific islands. They are restless foragers, constantly on the move and flicking their wings and tails, with many species showing glossy blue-black or metallic upperparts in the males.

children.name: Myiagra melvillensis
children.description: The Melville Flycatcher is endemic to Melville Island and Bathurst Island off northern Australia, with leaden-grey upperparts and pale underparts. It is sometimes considered a subspecies of the Leaden Flycatcher. It inhabits coastal forests and mangroves.

children.name: Symposiachrus
children.description: A genus of monarchs with striking black-and-white or boldly patterned plumage, found across the islands of Southeast Asia and the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Symposiachrus godeffroyi
children.description: The Yap Monarch is endemic to the island of Yap in Micronesia, with a black head, white throat, and yellow underparts. It inhabits forests and secondary growth, foraging for insects in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range and habitat loss.

children.name: Carterornis
children.description: A genus of strikingly coloured monarchs with white, yellow, or golden plumage and black masks or markings, found in Australia and the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Arses
children.description: A genus of small, strikingly patterned monarchs with frilled or ruffled neck feathers and pale blue eye-rings, found in lowland forests of New Guinea and surrounding islands.

children.name: Arses insularis
children.description: #REDIRECT Ochre-collared monarch Category:Taxa named by Adolf Bernhard Meyer.

children.name: Clytorhynchus
children.description: A genus of monarchs known as shrikebills, named for their stout, hooked bills adapted for tearing bark and searching for insects. They are found on islands of the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Metabolus
children.description: A monotypic genus of monarch restricted to the island of Chuuk in Micronesia. The single species is a striking bird with deep blue-black plumage and a distinctive white tail.

children.name: Eutrichomyias
children.description: A monotypic genus of monarch flycatcher found only on the island of Sangir in Indonesia. The single species is a stunning cerulean-blue bird with a long, forked tail and was once thought to be extinct.

children.name: Eutrichomyias rowleyi
children.description: #REDIRECT Cerulean flycatcher Category:Taxa named by Adolf Bernhard Meyer.

children.name: Neolalage
children.description: A monotypic genus of monarch flycatcher found in Vanuatu. The single species has a distinctive pale yellow belly and is often encountered in pairs or small family groups.

children.name: Chasiempis
children.description: A genus of three closely related monarch flycatchers endemic to the Hawaiian Islands, where they are known as elepaios. They are the only monarchs native to Hawaii and show remarkable variation between the islands in plumage and behaviour.

children.name: Pomarea
children.description: A genus of monarchs restricted to the islands of the south Pacific, with many species now critically endangered or extinct. They show a range of plumage colours from black to chestnut and white, and are named for the Pomarea Islands of French Polynesia.

children.name: Mayrornis
children.description: A genus of small monarchs restricted to the islands of Fiji and Tonga in the southwest Pacific. They have relatively plain plumage of grey, brown, and white, and are active, acrobatic foragers that frequently fan their tails.

children.name: Trochocercus
children.description: A genus of small crested monarchs found in the forests of sub-Saharan Africa. They are named for their distinctive erectile crests and are among the most colourful of African monarchs, with males showing glossy blue-black or slate-grey plumage.

children.name: Trochocercus cyanomelas
children.description: #REDIRECT Blue-mantled crested flycatcher.
